title: Input & Output
summary: Learn how to talk to your programs — print things to the screen and ask the user for information.

blocks.md: Every useful program needs two things: a way to **show information** to the user, and a way to **receive information** from the user. In Python, `print()` handles the first part and `input()` handles the second. Together they let you build programs that feel alive — programs that respond to the person using them.
You have already seen `print()` in action. At its simplest, you pass it a value and it displays that value on screen, then moves to the next line automatically.

blocks.caption: print() works with text, whole numbers, and decimals

blocks.md: You can pass **several values** to `print()` separated by commas. Python puts a space between each one by default.

blocks.caption: Commas separate the values; spaces appear automatically

blocks.md: That automatic space is controlled by a special keyword argument called `sep` (short for separator). You can change it to anything — a dash, a comma, or even nothing at all.

blocks.code:
print("2026", "07", "02", sep="-")
print("cat", "dog", "fish", sep=" | ")
print("a", "b", "c", sep="")
blocks.caption: sep lets you choose what goes between each value

blocks.md: By default `print()` adds a newline at the end, so the next print starts on a fresh line. You can change this with the `end` keyword argument. Setting `end=""` makes the next print continue on the **same line**.

blocks.code:
print("Loading", end="")
print(".", end="")
print(".", end="")
print(".")
blocks.caption: end="" removes the automatic newline

blocks.title: Think of print() like a typewriter
blocks.md: A typewriter prints characters and then **automatically moves to the next line** when you hit Enter. `end` is like deciding whether to hit Enter or not. `sep` is like choosing what you type between words.

`input()` pauses your program, shows a message (called a **prompt**), and waits for the user to type something and press Enter. Whatever they type is handed back to you as a value you can store in a variable.

blocks.code:
name = input("What is your name? ")
print("Nice to meet you,", name)
blocks.output:
What is your name? Alice
Nice to meet you, Alice
blocks.caption: input() always returns what the user typed

blocks.title: input() ALWAYS gives you a string
blocks.md:
Even if the user types `42`, Python gives you the **text** `"42"`, not the number `42`. If you try to do maths with it without converting, you will get a surprising result.

```python
age = input("How old are you? ")  # user types 20
print(age + 1)  # ERROR: can't add string and number
```

Always convert with `int()` or `float()` when you need a number.
Wrap `input()` in `int()` to get a whole number, or `float()` to get a decimal number. This is one of the most common patterns you will write.

blocks.code:
age = int(input("How old are you? "))
next_year = age + 1
print("Next year you will be", next_year)
blocks.output:
How old are you? 20
Next year you will be 21
blocks.caption: int() converts the string "20" into the number 20

blocks.md: An **f-string** lets you embed variables directly inside a string. Put an `f` before the opening quote and wrap your variable names in `{}`. Python replaces each `{}` with the variable's value. This is usually much neater than using commas in `print()`.

blocks.code:
name = "Maria"
age = 28
print(f"Hello, {name}! You are {age} years old.")
blocks.output: Hello, Maria! You are 28 years old.
blocks.caption: f-strings keep your output readable at a glance

blocks.md: You can even put simple expressions inside the `{}`.

blocks.caption: :.2f rounds the number to 2 decimal places inside the f-string

blocks.md: Here is a small interactive script that uses everything from this lesson: `input()`, type conversion, and an f-string for friendly output.

blocks.code:
name = input("What is your name? ")
year_born = int(input("What year were you born? "))
current_year = 2026
age = current_year - year_born
print(f"Hi {name}! You are around {age} years old.")
blocks.output:
What is your name? Sam
What year were you born? 1995
Hi Sam! You are around 31 years old.
blocks.caption: A complete interactive greeting program

blocks.title: Leave a space inside the prompt string
blocks.md: Write `input("Your name: ")` with a trailing space inside the quotes. Without it, the cursor appears right after the colon — `Your name:_` — which looks cramped and is harder to read.

blocks.question: A user types the number 5 when your program runs `score = input("Score: ")`. What is the value of `score` afterwards?
options: Nothing — input() does not return a value
blocks.explanation: input() always returns a string, no matter what the user types. To use it as a number you must convert it: int(score) or float(score).

keyTakeaways: print() can take multiple values separated by commas, with sep controlling what goes between them and end controlling the line ending.
input(prompt) pauses the program, shows a prompt, and returns whatever the user typed as a string.
Always convert input to int() or float() before doing any arithmetic with it.
F-strings (f"Hello, {name}") are the cleanest way to embed variables inside text.
A well-placed space at the end of your input prompt makes your program look polished.

exercise.prompt: Build a simple tip calculator. Ask the user for the meal cost (as a decimal number) and the tip percentage they want to leave (as a whole number). Then print the tip amount and the total to pay, both rounded to 2 decimal places. Use an f-string for the output.
exercise.starterCode:
meal = float(input("Meal cost ($): "))
tip_percent = int(input("Tip percentage (%): "))
# your code here
exercise.hint: Tip amount = meal * (tip_percent / 100). Total = meal + tip_amount. Use :.2f inside your f-string to format to 2 decimal places.
exercise.solution:
meal = float(input("Meal cost ($): "))
tip_percent = int(input("Tip percentage (%): "))
tip_amount = meal * (tip_percent / 100)
total = meal + tip_amount
print(f"Tip: ${tip_amount:.2f}")
print(f"Total: ${total:.2f}")
